Frequency Timing

How Often Should You Clean Your Windows in BC? The Shuswap Schedule Explained

Published 2026-05-13 · Wagon Windows

The standard advice — clean your windows twice a year — is reasonable for much of BC. In the Shuswap, it's the minimum. The combination of cottonwood pollen in spring, summer dust and lake spray, and fall leaf matter creates three distinct accumulation events that each leave a visible residue on glass. A three-clean schedule isn't upselling; it's a direct response to the local environment.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Get an Instant Estimate (403) 837-5092

BC-Wide Recommendation: Why Twice a Year Is the Starting Point

For most BC homeowners — particularly in urban or suburban settings where major environmental stressors are moderate — two professional window cleans per year is the standard recommendation. Spring and fall align with the two transition periods when glass accumulates the most: spring catches winter grime and early pollen; fall catches summer dust, cottonwood (for Interior BC locations), and pre-winter organic matter. This frequency maintains windows in acceptable condition without over-scheduling. For homes with modest window counts, no significant mineral issues, and a location away from agricultural dust, heavy tree cover, or lake spray, two cleans a year produces consistently presentable windows. What 'twice a year' doesn't account for is regional specificity. Lower Mainland homes face different conditions than Interior BC homes — more rainfall (which does some of the work for you on vertical glass) and different biological load. Shuswap homes face conditions that push the optimal frequency higher.

  • Lower Mainland: 2 cleans/year is typically sufficient
  • Okanagan (Kelowna, Vernon): 2–3 cleans, particularly if near agricultural operations
  • Shuswap region: 3 cleans recommended for most homes
  • Commercial properties: quarterly or more (see commercial section)

The Shuswap's Three-Season Accumulation Pattern

The Shuswap's specific combination of tree species, seasonal weather patterns, and agricultural proximity creates three distinct accumulation events that each merit a clean: Spring (April–June): Cottonwood pollen season. Cottonwood trees are prolific across Salmon Arm and the Shuswap Lake shoreline. The white seed fluff begins in late April and peaks through May into early June. It coats windows, loads into screen mesh, and mixes with fine pollen from birch and alder to create a dense layer on glass. A post-cottonwood clean in late May or early June removes this layer and starts the summer season with clear glass. Summer (July–August): Dust and lake spray. Summer brings dry, dusty conditions across Interior BC. Agricultural operations near Chase, Enderby, and Armstrong generate fine particulate that travels on summer winds. Combined with lake spray on Shuswap-facing properties and the general haze of a hot dry Interior BC summer, glass that was cleaned in May needs attention again by mid-summer, particularly for lake-view properties. Fall (September–October): Leaf matter and pre-winter organic residue. Deciduous trees shed fine surface matter before and during leaf drop — a fine organic film that builds on glass from mid-September. Cleaning in late September or October gives the home clean windows going into winter, which is particularly relevant for south and west-facing glass that gets raked by low winter sun angles.

  • Spring clean: late May–early June, after cottonwood peak
  • Summer clean: July–early August, before hosting season peaks
  • Fall clean: late September–mid October, before first freeze

How Lake Proximity Changes the Frequency Recommendation

For properties on or near Shuswap Lake — in Blind Bay, Sorrento, along the east and south arms of the lake, and in Sicamous — the recommended clean frequency is higher than for Salmon Arm inland properties. The reasons are the same as those driving hard water staining: lake water carries dissolved minerals, wind-driven spray deposits them on glass, and each evaporation cycle adds to the accumulation. On a wind-exposed lakefront property in Blind Bay, a spring clean may be partially undone within four to six weeks by summer lake spray. For lake-adjacent homes, four cleans per year is defensible — spring, midsummer, late summer, and fall — particularly if large picture windows face the water. The economics still work: $229–$299 per clean on a medium lake home adds up to $900–$1,200 annually, against the cost of hard water etching that requires treatment or glass replacement. An alternative for lake properties is three professional cleans with interim DIY spot maintenance on the most-exposed windows between visits. A daily or weekly wipe of lake-facing glass with a squeegee and clean water prevents mineral accumulation without the cost of full professional visits.

  • Lake-adjacent (within ~300m of shore): 3–4 professional cleans/year
  • Severe exposure (directly lakefront, wind-exposed): consider 4 cleans
  • Interim DIY maintenance on lake-facing glass between professional visits is cost-effective

Commercial Window Cleaning Frequency vs Residential

Commercial properties in Salmon Arm — storefronts, professional offices, restaurants, hospitality businesses — require more frequent window cleaning than residential homes. The reasons are customer perception, higher traffic generating more proximity grime, and regulatory cleanliness expectations in food service environments. Most commercial storefronts in Salmon Arm's downtown area benefit from monthly exterior cleaning during the operating season (April–October) and bimonthly through winter. High-traffic retail like restaurants and cafes on the main commercial strip often benefit from weekly cleaning, as hand smudges, exhaust, and foot-traffic spray accumulate visibly within days. Hospitality businesses — hotels, Airbnbs, vacation rentals — have a different frequency driver: guest arrival. Windows cleaned the week before a rental season opens matter more than adherence to a calendar schedule. Wagon Windows works with commercial clients on schedules tailored to their operations. For commercial properties near agricultural dust sources (relevant for businesses in Enderby and Armstrong particularly), the summer frequency may need to be higher to maintain a presentable street presence during peak tourist season.

Annual Scheduling Strategy for Shuswap Homeowners

The most effective scheduling approach for Shuswap residential homeowners is to plan all three cleans at the start of the year, securing preferred dates in advance rather than trying to book reactively after accumulation is already visible. Spring fills fastest and books earliest. March or early April is the right time to lock in a late May or early June spring clean. By the time cottonwood is coating everything and you're motivated to call, the good dates are gone. Summer is more flexible but still benefits from advance booking, particularly for lake-view properties where demand is higher. Target late July for a summer clean if you're hosting or have an upcoming reason to want the windows looking their best. Fall scheduling can happen in August or September. The target window for fall cleaning is September 15–October 15 in Salmon Arm — late enough for most leaf matter to have fallen from deciduous trees, early enough to complete before reliable freezing temperatures make exterior cleaning impractical. Call (403) 837-5092 or book all three visits via the Calendly link at wagonwindow.com. Setting up recurring annual service means you don't have to think about it — the dates are in the calendar and the work gets done.

  • March–April: book spring clean (late May–early June slot)
  • June–July: book summer clean (late July slot)
  • August–September: book fall clean (Sept 15–Oct 15 slot)

Frequently Asked Questions

Is twice a year enough for a Shuswap home?

Twice a year is a minimum. For most Shuswap homes — particularly those with any tree cover, lake exposure, or proximity to agricultural dust — three cleans (spring, summer, fall) produces noticeably better results year-round. Lake-adjacent homes benefit from four.

What's the minimum frequency to prevent hard water etching on lake-facing windows?

Annual treatment is the minimum for lake-adjacent windows. Biannual is better. The goal is removing mineral deposits before they have multiple seasons to bond to the glass permanently.

Does rain clean windows enough to reduce professional cleaning frequency?

Not in the Shuswap. Interior BC's summer is too dry for meaningful rain cleaning during the peak accumulation months. Spring rain can temporarily improve appearances while still leaving mineral residue. Rain on dirty windows creates streaks, not clean glass.

Can I schedule multiple cleans per year with Wagon Windows in advance?

Yes. Booking all three annual cleans at once is the easiest way to secure preferred dates — particularly for spring, which fills earliest. Call (403) 837-5092 or book via wagonwindow.com.
